title: What AI Can Do
description: Explore the capabilities and limitations of modern AI — what it excels at, where it struggles, and how to think about AI as a tool in your toolkit.
learningObjectives: Identify the types of tasks where AI assistants like Claude are most effective and reliable
Recognize the current limitations of AI and situations where human judgment is essential
Develop a practical mental model for deciding when and how to use AI as a tool

sections.title: What Is Generative AI?
sections.content:
You have probably heard the term "artificial intelligence" everywhere lately. But what does it actually mean when people talk about AI tools like Claude, and how is it different from the AI you might have seen in movies?

**AI is software that recognizes patterns.** At its core, modern AI is software that has been trained on enormous amounts of text — books, articles, websites, conversations, and code — to recognize patterns in language. When you ask Claude a question, it draws on those patterns to generate a helpful response. Think of it like someone who has read an entire library and can discuss what they have learned, but who has never actually experienced the world firsthand.

**Generative AI creates new content.** The "generative" part means these AI systems produce new text, code, or ideas rather than just looking up stored answers. When Claude writes an email for you or explains a concept, it is constructing that response word by word based on its understanding of language and knowledge. It is not copying and pasting from a database — it is generating something new each time.

**AI is not thinking the way humans do.** This is an important distinction. Claude processes language using mathematical patterns, not through understanding or consciousness. It does not have feelings, beliefs, or personal experiences. When Claude says "I think" or "I believe," it is using conversational language patterns, not expressing genuine opinions. Understanding this helps you use AI more effectively because you will know what kinds of questions and tasks play to its strengths.

**Why this matters for you.** Whether you are a student, a professional, a creative, or just someone curious about technology, understanding what AI actually is will help you use it effectively. You do not need a computer science degree — you just need a clear picture of what the tool can and cannot do, which is exactly what this module will give you.

sections.title: What Claude Is Good At
sections.content:
Claude has genuine strengths that can save you time and help you think through problems. Knowing these strengths helps you reach for AI when it will actually help rather than when it might get in the way.

**Writing and communication.** Claude excels at drafting, editing, and refining written content. This includes emails, reports, essays, social media posts, cover letters, and more. It can adjust tone (formal vs. casual), improve clarity, fix grammar, and restructure arguments. If you have ever stared at a blank page, Claude can help you get a solid first draft in seconds.

**Analysis and reasoning.** Give Claude a block of text, data, or a complex situation, and it can help you break it down. It can summarize long documents, identify key themes, compare options, and walk through logical arguments step by step. This makes it a powerful thinking partner when you need to make sense of information.

**Coding and technical tasks.** Even if you are not a programmer, it is worth knowing that Claude can write, explain, and debug code in dozens of programming languages. It can also help with tasks like writing formulas for spreadsheets, creating structured data, or explaining technical concepts in plain language.

**Brainstorming and creativity.** Need ideas for a project, a gift, a business name, or a new approach to a problem? Claude can generate lists of ideas, explore different angles, and help you think outside your usual patterns. It will not replace your creativity, but it can be a great springboard.

**Explanation and teaching.** Claude can explain almost any topic at different levels of complexity. You can ask it to explain quantum physics like you are five years old, or to give you a graduate-level explanation of the same concept. This makes it a versatile learning tool for picking up new subjects.

playground.description: Try one of Claude's core strengths — turning rough ideas into polished writing. Give it a short, messy draft and see how it transforms your writing while keeping your meaning intact.
playground.systemPrompt: You are a friendly writing assistant. Take the user's rough draft and rewrite it as a clear, polished paragraph. Keep the same meaning and tone, but improve grammar, flow, and clarity. After the rewrite, briefly explain 2-3 specific changes you made and why.
playground.userMessage: Can you clean up this rough draft for me? Here it is: "So basically our team did pretty good this quarter. Sales were up alot and customers seemed more happy than before. We still got some stuff to work on tho like response times which arent great and also we need to figure out the new software situation."

sections.title: What AI Struggles With
sections.content:
Just as important as knowing what Claude is good at is understanding where it falls short. Every tool has limitations, and being aware of these will save you from frustration and mistakes.

**Real-time and current information.** Claude's knowledge comes from its training data, which has a cutoff date. It does not browse the internet in real time (unless given tools to do so), so it may not know about yesterday's news, the latest stock prices, or a new product release. Always double-check when you need information that changes frequently.

**Perfect accuracy and factual precision.** Claude can sometimes present incorrect information confidently — a phenomenon called "hallucination." It might invent a plausible-sounding book title, cite a study that does not exist, or get a specific date wrong. This does not mean Claude is unreliable, but it does mean you should verify important facts, especially for anything that carries real consequences.

**Emotional intelligence and personal advice.** While Claude can discuss emotions and offer thoughtful perspectives, it does not truly understand what you are going through. It cannot replace a therapist, a trusted friend, or a mentor who knows your specific situation. Use it as a starting point for thinking, not as a substitute for genuine human connection on sensitive personal matters.

**Mathematics and precise calculations.** While Claude can handle many math problems, it occasionally makes arithmetic errors, especially with complex calculations. For anything where precision matters — financial calculations, engineering measurements, statistical analysis — use a calculator or spreadsheet to verify Claude's math.

**Subjective judgment and personal taste.** Claude can tell you the pros and cons of two options, but it cannot know which one you will actually enjoy more. Questions like "which city should I move to?" or "will I like this book?" require personal knowledge that Claude simply does not have about you. It can help you organize your thinking, but the final call is always yours.

playground.description: See AI limitations firsthand. Ask Claude a question that requires very recent information and notice how it handles the gap. This builds your instinct for when to trust AI output and when to verify.
playground.systemPrompt: You are a helpful assistant. Answer the user's question as best you can. If you are unsure or the question requires information you might not have, be transparent about your uncertainty rather than guessing.
playground.userMessage: What were the top 3 highest-grossing movies at the box office last weekend? Include the exact dollar amounts for each.

sections.title: AI as a Tool, Not a Replacement
sections.content:
The most productive way to think about AI is as a powerful tool in your toolkit — like a calculator, a search engine, or a spell checker, but more versatile. It amplifies what you can do, but it does not replace your judgment, creativity, or expertise.

**The partnership model.** The best results come from working with Claude, not handing everything off to it. You bring the context, the goals, and the judgment. Claude brings speed, breadth of knowledge, and the ability to process information quickly. Together, you can accomplish more than either could alone. For example, you might ask Claude to draft an email, but you review it to make sure it sounds like you and hits the right points.

**When to reach for AI.** A good rule of thumb: use AI when you need a first draft, a second opinion, a brainstorming partner, or help processing information. These are tasks where speed matters, where you want to explore options, or where you have enough expertise to evaluate the output. If you are writing a report and know the subject well, Claude can save you an hour on the first draft. If you know nothing about the subject, Claude's draft might contain errors you cannot spot.

**When to rely on yourself (or other tools).** Keep your own judgment in the driver's seat for decisions that have significant consequences, require personal or emotional understanding, need up-to-the-minute accuracy, or involve specialized expertise where errors could be harmful. A good question to ask yourself is: "If Claude gets this wrong, what are the consequences?" If the answer is "not much," lean on AI freely. If the answer is "it could be a real problem," verify everything carefully.

**Building good habits from the start.** As you begin using AI tools, develop the habit of reviewing AI output before using it. Think of it like proofreading — you would not send an important email without reading it first, and you should not use AI-generated content without checking it either. This habit will serve you well as AI tools become more integrated into everyday work.

sections.title: Identify Good vs. Bad AI Use Cases
sections.content: Now it is time to practice your judgment about when AI is a good fit and when it is not.

exercise.prompt:
Below is a list of tasks. For each one, decide whether it is a GOOD use case for AI, a BAD use case for AI, or somewhere in between. Explain your reasoning for each.

1. Writing a first draft of a thank-you email to a colleague
2. Diagnosing a medical condition based on symptoms
3. Brainstorming names for a new pet
4. Calculating your exact tax liability for the year
5. Summarizing a 20-page research paper
6. Deciding whether to accept a job offer
7. Explaining what a Python error message means
8. Writing a legal contract for a business deal

For each task, state whether it is a good, bad, or mixed use case and explain WHY based on what you learned about AI's strengths and limitations.
hints: Think about the consequences if the AI gives an incorrect answer — how serious would the impact be?
Consider whether you or someone else could easily verify the AI's output
Ask yourself whether the task requires real-time information, specialized expertise, or personal judgment
Some tasks might be good for getting a starting point but bad for getting the final answer
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should correctly identify that tasks like drafting emails (1), brainstorming pet names (3), summarizing papers (5), and explaining error messages (7) are good AI use cases because they are low-stakes, verifiable, or play to AI's strengths in language and analysis. Medical diagnosis (2) and legal contracts (8) should be identified as bad or risky because errors can have serious consequences and they require licensed professional expertise. Tax calculation (4) is bad because it requires precision and current tax law. Job offer decisions (6) are bad because they require personal judgment about life circumstances. For each item, the reasoning should reference specific AI strengths or limitations. Partial credit for answers that are in the right direction but lack clear reasoning.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Here is what you should remember about what AI can and cannot do:

• **AI is a pattern-matching tool, not a thinking being.** Claude generates responses based on patterns learned from vast amounts of text. It does not understand the world the way you do, but it can process and generate language remarkably well.

• **Know the strengths.** Claude excels at writing, analysis, brainstorming, coding, and explanation. These are tasks where speed and breadth of knowledge matter, and where you can verify the output.

• **Know the limitations.** Claude struggles with real-time information, perfect accuracy, emotional understanding, and precise calculations. Never rely on it blindly for high-stakes decisions.

• **Think of AI as a partner, not a replacement.** The best results come from combining your judgment and context with Claude's speed and versatility. You drive — Claude helps navigate.

• **Build the habit of checking.** Always review AI output before using it, especially when accuracy matters. The more consequential the task, the more carefully you should verify.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content: Before you move on, put what you have learned into practice by thinking through a real scenario.

exercise.prompt: Imagine you are planning a weekend trip to a city you have never visited. Describe three specific ways you could use Claude to help you plan, and one part of the planning where you should NOT rely on AI. For each, explain your reasoning based on what you learned about AI strengths and limitations.
hints: Think about the different planning stages — research, logistics, decision-making, and personal preferences
Consider which parts of trip planning involve factual information versus personal taste
Ask yourself: for each task, could you easily verify whether Claude's suggestions are accurate?
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should identify at least three realistic uses of AI for trip planning that align with known strengths, such as generating itinerary ideas, summarizing reviews of neighborhoods or attractions, drafting packing lists, or explaining local customs. The limitation identified should connect to a genuine AI weakness — for example, that Claude cannot confirm real-time availability, current prices, or operating hours, or that it cannot know the learner's personal preferences for food, pace, or budget. Strong answers will show clear reasoning linking each suggestion back to a specific strength or limitation discussed in the module.
